coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot incorporation in the zeolitic imidazolate framework with sodalite (SOD) topology: Synthesis and improving the adsorption ability in liquid phase
coredata.dc:creator: Rabeie, Bahareh
Mahkam, Mehrdad
Mahmoodi, Niyaz Mohammad
Lan, Christopher Q.
coredata.dc:description: An amazing combination of zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with three-dimensional metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) improves the resulting nanocomposite properties. Herein, The GQD was incorporated in the ZIF-8 surface, to emphasize another application of GQD that has been less considered before. The characteristics of synthesized nanomaterials were confirmed by FTIR, XRD, TGA, BET, Raman, SEM, and HRTEM. The removal of malachite green (MG) was investigated to compare the adsorption properties of nanocomposite with pristine GQD and ZIF-8. The effects of GQD loading dose in composite (2–20%), adsorbents dosage (0.7–2.5 mg), initial dye concentration (20–50 mg/L), and the pH of MG solution (4−7) as the influential parameters on pollutant removal were investigated. The dye adsorption capacity of hybrid nanocomposite (2500 mg/g) was reasonably better than pure GQD (294 mg/g) and MOF (1111 mg/g). Besides, this process was in good agreement with the pseudo-second-order kinetics and the Langmuir isotherm model

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot incorporation in the zeolitic imidazolate framework with sodalite (SOD) topology: Synthesis and improving the adsorption ability in liquid phase

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An amazing combination of zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with three-dimensional metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) improves the resulting nanocomposite properties. Herein, The GQD was incorporated in the ZIF-8 surface, to emphasize another application of GQD that has been less considered before. The characteristics of synthesized nanomaterials were confirmed by FTIR, XRD, TGA, BET, Raman, SEM, and HRTEM. The removal of malachite green (MG) was investigated to compare the adsorption properties of nanocomposite with pristine GQD and ZIF-8. The effects of GQD loading dose in composite (2–20%), adsorbents dosage (0.7–2.5 mg), initial dye concentration (20–50 mg/L), and the pH of MG solution (4−7) as the influential parameters on pollutant removal were investigated. The dye adsorption capacity of hybrid nanocomposite (2500 mg/g) was reasonably better than pure GQD (294 mg/g) and MOF (1111 mg/g). Besides, this process was in good agreement with the pseudo-second-order kinetics and the Langmuir isotherm model

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, water contamination has become a mounting concern for officials, environmental activists, industrialists, and even ordinary people. This crisis has posed a severe menace not only to humankind's health but also to marine life and other organisms. Despite the hazardous, carcinogenic, and mutagenic properties of dyes, due to their various applications and positive effects on the human psyche, we witness widespread use of them in various industries including textiles, cosmetics, optics, printing, plastics, paper, and leather. Therefore, they have become one of the major contributors to water pollution [1,2]. In wastewater treatment, numerous techniques have been proposed to eliminate dyes, as an instance can mention to chemical methods (chemical precipitation, coagulation, flocculation, advanced oxidation, electrolysis, etc.), physical methods (membrane filtration, ion exchange, adsorption, etc.) and biochemical methods (algae degradation, aerobic-anaerobic combination (conventional method), fungal cultures, microbial cultures such as mixed bacterial, etc.) [3–18]. Among all processing technologies, adsorption as an effective prominent choice concerning significant cost saving, simplicity of sketching, easy use without secondary pollution, energy efficiency, as well environmentally friendly has been favorably considered [1,7,8]. Malachite green (MG) whose removal was studied, is a water-soluble cationic dye, belongs to the triphenylmethane category which has been used as antiseptic and fungicidal for human, antibacterial, antifungal in aquaculture and commercial fish hatchery industry, protozoan infections controlling in the paper, food, textile industries. Despite all these uses, malachite green in the water can motive many complications such as respiratory toxicity, fractures, or mutagenesis of chromosomal and carcinogenesis [9]. Comparing conventional adsorbents, inorganic-organic hybrid materials have found a special place in recent research. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are constructed by metal or clusters-based nodes coordinated to the tunable organic bridge for forming one, two, or three-dimensional structures [8,12]. These compounds are also called porous coordination polymers (PCPs), which have emerged with excellent properties because of their well-ordered porosity, adjustable structures, exceptional high surface area, chemical tenability, allowing them to control their pore size and shape [8,12-15,22]. Their effective application such as separation [23–25], drug delivery and biomedicine [21,26,27], chemical sensors [19,20], heterogeneous catalysis [28–31] indicate their increasing importance.

Zeolitic imidazole frameworks (ZIFs), a combination of the worthwhile properties of zeolites and MOFs, as a momentous subset of metal-organic frameworks, are considered where Zn/Co center metal ions bridged by N atoms of imidazolate ligands. Owing to permanent microporosity, transition metal or cluster nodes thermal and chemical stability, impressive surface area, appropriate linkers and, flexible opening with tailorable cavities 11.6 Å that are accessible through small apertures with diameters of 3.4 Å, and it has a cubic space group with unit cell dimensions of 16.32 Å, make them a promising choice for efficient adsorbent [16,32–35].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional, single, or few-layer graphene materials with the thin graphitic plane (< 2 nm deep) and sidelong dimensions generally < 10 nm, which have become research hotspot over the last decade [36–40]. Because of their excellent structure with a bunch of hydroxyl and carboxylic and other functional groups on their surface, dispersibility in water, low toxicity, low-cost production methods, low photobleaching, biocompatibility, good photostability, and chemical inertness are the significant choice in many applications [36,39–41]. There are two broad methods for synthesizing GQDs: top-down and bottom-up approaches. It is marvelous to note that the size of synthetic materials, their thickness, the chemical groups, and the configuration of edges depend on these methods [42,43]. These zero-dimensional materials have appeared with a wide range of amazing physicochemical properties (electrochemical, catalytic, electronic, optical, etc.), although some of these features may be similar to their two-dimensional counterparts, low size, very thin thickness, diverse functional groups, the talent of heteroatomic dopantes make them incredibly capable of improving and creating new applications and benefits [44]. Dye adsorption from wastewater is a wondrous application for graphene quantum dots recently is trying to be well introduced [37]. Adding a punch of new functional groups with high-performance quantum structure capability can be an impressive step to improve the resulting hybrid properties and even create new and effective features. In this paper, by encapsulating graphene quantum dots in ZIF-8, an attempt is made to emphasize the different uses of GQD that has been less discussed in the past.

To date, an abundance of approaches has been investigated to incorporate functional species with MOFs to obtain modified composite materials with enhanced properties [36,39,45–48]. Several ZIFs composites were used for removing dyes ( Table 1) [49–58].

Methanol (99.9% synthetic grade), and 2-Methylimidazole (2-MIM - 99% synthetic grade) were purchased from Merck Company. Zinc nitrate (98% reagent grade) and citric acid (99.5% (were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Also, Malachite Green from the Ciba brand was used ( Table 2). To adjust the pH values, the diluted NaOH, and HCl solution were prepared. The distilled water was used for all aqueous solutions. ( Fig. 1).

All adsorption test samples contained 100 mL of defined concentration (20–50 mg/L) of MG solution, which was stirred with a constant speed of 700 rpm at ambient temperature. To investigate the initial solution pH effect on MG adsorption reaction, dilute HCl and NaOH were used for the range of (pH=4–7). To obtain the optimum dose of adsorbent, different amounts (0.7–2.5 mg) of adsorbent in100 mL of 20 mg/L MG solution at pH= 7 at ambient temperature were studied. By centrifugation, the adsorbent containing adsorbed dye was separated.

After determining of MG concentration, the percentage of dye removal by the adsorbents were obtained by this equation:

To study the functional groups of the synthesized materials, FTIR spectroscopy in the domain of 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 was used. The nanomaterials morphology was determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) LEO 1455VP. To verify the material crystalline structure, the XRD model Siemens D-5000 diffractometer with Cu Kαradiation (λ = 1.5406 A°) was used. For GQD synthesis, Microwave equipment (SynthWAVE, Italy) was used. Thermogravimetric analyses of the synthesized materials were investigated by a Q50 TGA instrument at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area and materials pore volume measurement were done through N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms by a micrometric surface area and porosity analyzer (ASAP-2020). High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images were provided by American FEI TEC9G20 (Vmax=200Kv). To measure the residual MG concentration in the sample solutions, the double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) was used.

To control the synthesized GQD@ZIF-8 and its precursor structure, the FTIR spectra were studied in the range 4000–500 cm−1 in Fig. 2. The GQD spectrum shows a significant wide peak at 3433 cm−1, which is related to -OH stretching. Another wide peak at 1715 cm−1 indicates CO stretching, and two peaks at 1510, and 1629 cm−1 are observed because of CC stretching. C-O stretching at 1389 cm−1, C-O-C stretching at 1042 cm−1 are appeared [36,64,65]. The FTIR of ZIF-8 has a good fit compared to previously reported data. The aliphatic and aromatic C-H vibrations at 3133 and 2962, 2928 cm−1 are observed. A peak at 1588 cm−1 was ascribed to CN. At 1457, and 1420 cm−1 CC stretching appeared. The wide peak at 3430 cm−1 shows -OH group, which can confirm the presence of water [49,50,56]. In the GQD@ZIF-8 spectrum, the band at 3419 cm−1 indicates –OH stretching vibration. The aliphatic and aromatic C-H vibrations appeared at 3133, 2960, and 2929 cm−1. Bands at1029–1243 cm−1 are attributed to C–O stretching which confirms that the GQD surface possesses functional groups containing oxygen. The peaks at 1458–1310 cm−1 have belonged to H-MIM. The CN stretch appears at 1585 cm−1. At 1714 cm−1 wide peak indicates CO presence at GQD surface. The wide peak at 1714 cm−1 fits well with CO stretching of GQD [36,39,66].

Raman spectroscopy is an effective tool in the field of research by inquiring about molecular vibrations. Pristine MOF and its composite were irradiated at 532 nm by laser ( Fig. 3). As can be seen in their spectrum, the peaks of 176.15 and 397 cm- can be attributed to the Zn-O and Zn-O-Zn vibrations as well as the possible incorporation of the hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the GQD surface and Zn (II). The majority of peaks available in the Raman spectrum are related to the imidazole rings and the methyl group attached to them. Strong peaks in the range of 686 cm, 1148 cm, and 1461 cm- are attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the imidazole ring, as well as C-N stretching vibration, and vibration of the methyl group attached to these rings. In addition, in the range of 2950 cm, anti-symmetric stretching vibrations of the C-H methyl group and at 3200 stretching vibrations of C-H in the imidazole ring have appeared [67–69]. Also, two sharp peaks in 1146 cm- (Dband) and 1460 cm- (Gband) can be related to the vibrations of graphical network(carbon sp2) and defects in the chaotic regions of this network. The ID/IG ratio for the composite was 0.819 and the intact MOF was 0.712, which confirms the presence of amorphous graphene on the composite surface [70].

G-band of GQDs in the range of 1590 cm-, portends the vibrational state of E2g aromatic area in the hexagonal lattice, as well as band D in the range of 1350 following defects in the performance of surface functional groups. Indeed synthesis process may be annihilated this band. the ID / IG ratio is used to assess the degree of GQDs defects. The Raman scattering of GQDs depends on their size, to the extent that the G-band frequency increases with increment size [71,72].

The GQD@ZIF-8 hybrid nanocomposite, ZIF-8, and GQD crystalline structures are demonstrated by XRD patterns in Fig. 4. The orderly porous crystal structure of ZIF-8 and nanocomposite particles is observed between 2θ values of 0–60 degrees. The relative magnitude and prominent peak positions are as in previous reports. Peaks at 2θ = 7.42º (011), 10.52º (002), 12.9º (112), 14.83º (022), 16.52º (013), 18.19º (222), 22.16º (114), 24.58º (233), 25.7º (224), 26.77º (134), 29.9º (044), 30.85º (244), and 31.76º (235) are the confirmation of ZIF-8 structure [39,64,73]. Due to its concordance with previous literature, the GQD@ZIF-8 hybrid nanocomposite XRD pattern clearly shows the presence of GQD in the ZIF-8 diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20–30º which confirms successful synthesis of hybrid nanocomposite [36,39]. As seen in the wide-spread XRD patterns (2c,2d), for the composite with higher GQD content, the presence of graphene quantum dot in the ZIF-8 crystallin structure is quite evident. The placement of amorphous GQD in the ventricle of ZIF caused ZIF-8 peaks to change and wide in the range of 2θ = 20–40º. Despite the different morphology of pure ZIF-8 and its nanocomposites (note in SEM and TEM images), no fundamental difference was observed in their XRD pattern, which was consistent with the reports of previous articles [73].

As can be seen in Fig. 5, the surface morphology and size of the pristine ZIF-8 and GQD@ZIF-8 were studied by SEM. Interestingly, the incorporation of GQDs in the ZIF-8 structure has changed the external morphology of the ZIF-8 from cube to rhombic dodecahedral. Although the method of preparing composites using PVP is common [74–78], According to the findings of Xuechao Cai and his colleagues, PVP may be a reason for this morphological change [79]. he similar case has been proposed to control the morphology of MOFs [79–81]. In the synthesis method, it was mentioned GQD first settles on zinc salts, then imidazolate ligands were added. Thus, GQDs are aggregated and distributed spontaneously during the growth process. The process of encapsulation occurs by both physical and chemical adsorption. Physical adsorption happens in the ZIF-8, and the interaction of GQDs with the zinc ions forms chemical adsorption. GQD@ZIF-8 crystals with nanoscale size and rhombic dodecahedral morphology are created [36,39,49,82]. Confirmatory SEM observations have been found in many articles in which the type of solvent [83], source of zinc [73], the molar ratio of ingredients [84], as well as the synthesis method [85] are mentioned as factors influencing the morphology of ZIF-8 [86].

HRTEM images of the GQD in Fig. 5(a) reveal that the GQD consists of somewhat circular shape monodisperse nanoparticles and graphical structure[37,59,60]. Comparing HRTEM images of GQD@ ZIF-8 and pristine ZIF-8 ( Fig. 6(e), f, i, j) indicated that although the morphology of the nanocomposite and pristine ZIF-8 has changed at the growth rate of the crystal surface, and the structure change from cubic to rhombic dodecahedral is observed, but especially in composites with lower GQD content, the architectural structure of MOF after exposure GQDs at the nanocomposite surface is partially preserved, which is also confirmed by the XRD patterns in Fig. 3. However, in composite with higher GQDs content, due to GQDs agglomeration, the MOF surface becomes somewhat rough, but the shape of GQD@ZIF-8 architecture remains the same. Fig. 5(c, d, e, f, j, h, i, j) shows GQD embedded in the nanocomposite, which verifies the useful junction of GQD in the prepared composite structure. In Fig. 6(g, h, i, j) for composite with higher graphene quantum dot content, the agglomeration of GQD is seen. The aforementioned interpretation confirms that the GQD@ZIF-8 composite is successfully synthesized [36,39,82,87]. Also, according to Fig. 6(k), the histogram diagram of the distribution of GQDs in the nanocomposite, the uniformity of GQDs distribution with an average size of 3.5–8.3 nm in GQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was confirmed. Considering the larger size of GQD compared to ZIF-8 pores (Table S2 data), it was concluded that GQDs are located on the surface of ZIF-8 [88–90].

The thermal stability of the synthesized materials was demonstrated using thermogravimetric analysis. As it can be seen at TGA curves (Supplementary file: Fig. S3), hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups and water adsorption cause thermal instability of GQD. According to previous literature, the significant mass loss of 6.3%, 47.4%, 34.4%, and 11.7% at 110, 170, 230, and 588 °C, respectively, can be due to losing hydroxyl, oxygen-containing groups in the graphene quantum dot surface and physically adsorbed water [36,49,66]. The TGA curves of ZIF-8 indicate a 3D lattice of MOF and high thermal stability until 395 °C. Before the inception of the exothermic decomposition of the imidazolate ligands as a bridging, TGA shows no considerable weight loss. The most significant mass loss is observed up to 395 °C, which results from Zn(NO3)2. A sharp step of 64.7% reveals the thermal decomposition of ZIF-8 nanocrystals in the temperature range of 400–610 °C. This weight loss indicated that zinc oxide emerged as the final computing product of ZIF-8 nanocrystals [36,49,73]. The TG analysis of GQD@ZIF-8 demonstrated a 9% weight loss between 150 °C and 250 °C, due to the release of some GQDs hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [36,66]. Another weight loss at 480–730 °C possibly can be attributed to carbonization and the exothermic decomposition process of the ZIF-8 structure [36,49,66,91].

The BET method can describe specific surface areas, porosity, and the adsorption capacity of our adsorbents (Supplementary file: Fig. S4, Table S2). Knowing that MOFs can have various sizes of microporous, from "ultra micropores" (p < 7%) to "super micropores" (7

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of influential parameters on MG removal

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of MG solution concentration on equilibrium adsorption capacity was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7, by enhancing the initial concentration of the malachite green solution, dye removal decreases. It justifies that at lower MG concentration, the adsorption process is well balanced due to the strong adsorption of the dye to the acting sites of the adsorbents and the proper proportion of the number of MG molecules to these active sites [99,100].

The effect of GQD content at nanocomposite for dye removal and the equilibrium adsorption capacity were evaluated, and the results can be seen in Fig. 8. The GQD@ZIF-8 composite exhibits better dye adsorption capacity than its precursor. GQD@ZIF-8 4%Wt as the optimum amount of GQD rationally showed admissible adsorption capacity. However, as the GQD content in the composite increases, adsorption capacity decreases. This can be justified by the agglomeration of GQD in the ZIF-8 composite surface with high GQD ratios. In GQD @ ZIF-8 composites, due to the presence of GQD, electrostatic interactions, π–π stacking interaction, and hydrogen bonding affect the adsorption interactions, but it is thought that the interaction of the aromatic imidazole rings with the MG rings, π–π stacking interaction, is one of the main factors for MG adsorption capacity [37,49]. The functional groups of GQD and its quantum size should not be underestimated [41,43]. In addition, the growth of different crystal surfaces in the structure of MOFs has been introduced as one of the momentous reasons for the functional differences of various MOF morphologies. The different growth of the crystal surface in the composite may be another reason for the different adsorption rates [80,101,102].

To assess the adsorbent dose effect, it was varied in the ranges of 0.7–2.5 mg. We prepared the reactors of (20 mg/L) Malachite Green solution, which was stirred at room temperature for 100 min Fig. 9 shows the MG removal rate (R %) and qe value at different adsorbent amounts. As the results show, although increasing the adsorbent dose increased the MG adsorption, a reverse trend is observed for equilibrium adsorption capacity. Whatever the adsorbent dose increases, adsorbent surface active sites will be more accessible [49,103].

Lower adsorption capacity with increasing adsorbent dose may be due to some kind of struggle for adsorption between the adsorbate molecules and getting cleavage for the concentration gradient [49,104].

To find out more about the effect of protonation and deprotonation of the synthesized adsorbents in the adsorption process, the removal rate was investigated at several pHs. The studies were performed in the pH range of 4–7, which can be seen in Fig. 10. This issue can be interpreted as: from pH = 7 to pH = 4, qe (mg/g) appears with a slightly decreasing trend without significant change [104,105]. In acidic conditions, the adsorbent surface encounters proton accumulation, and since the reported zero point charge (PHPZC) for ZIF-8 is pH> 9.5, this MOF at the evaluated pH has a positive charge. The ZIF-8 Zeta potential starts to become negative at pH> 9.5. Also, On the one hand, malachite green with(pKa = 10.3) in acidic conditions has a high positive charge density, so with the increasing number of the positive charge of active sites, electrostatic repulsion Between the MG molecule and adsorbents surface occurs. on the other hand, excess H + in solution reduces adsorption by competing with cationic dye malachite green [106,107].

Several different isotherm models, including the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Tempkin were utilized to describe our acquired data and relationships between the amount of MG adsorbed by the adsorbents (ZIF-8 and GQD@ZIF-8 and GQD) at ambient temperature (Fig. S1: Supplementary file). The surface features, adsorbent affinity, and adsorption mechanism are explained by the mentioned adsorption isotherms. The Langmuir isotherm emphasizes the assumption that the adsorption trend is naturally monolayer, and the contaminants can be adsorbed as a layer on the adsorbent surface. According to the Langmuir isotherm, adsorption and desorption between the adsorbent and solution can occur homogeneously, and specific sites on the adsorbent surface and the relation between the dye solution and the adsorbent was described as reversible chemical equilibrium, but there is no transition at the surface plate. The linear Langmuir isotherm is as follow [108]:

Considering a higher R2 value (0.919,0.944 and 0.916), it has been confirmed that the dye adsorption by all three adsorbents follows the Langmuir isotherms. The results were summarized in
Table 3 .

Adsorption kinetics research presents valuable information on the mechanism of surface adsorption process control, diffusion mechanism, and chemical reaction [49,108]. Since the kinetic models are influenced by the physical and chemical properties of the adsorbents, these properties play a decisive role in evaluating the factors affecting the reaction rate [110]. With regards to the intraparticle diffusion kinetics, pseudo-first-order, and pseudo-second-order, the malachite green adsorption on the synthesized adsorbents was evaluated (Fig. S2: Supplementary file).

For reversible adsorption on solid surfaces, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model was exhibited. The linearized pseudo-first-order equation is presented below [111]:

Herein the solute molecules, first move from the liquid to the adsorbent surface, then penetrate their pores, which diffusion step is slower and determines the rate of adsorption. So the adsorption process can be essentially multi-step. To determine the diffusion mechanism, evaluating results with the intraparticle diffusion model can be desirable. + I

Considering a higher R2 value (0.998vs. 0.996 and 0.993), it has been confirmed that the pseudo-second-order model is preferable for our experiment. The results were summarized in Table S1 (Supplementary file). Based on the obtained results, the chemisorption process is a crucial step to control the MG adsorption rate by the prepared composite.

Electrostatic interactions, coordination interaction, hydrophobicity, π–π stacking interaction, and hydrogen bonding are mechanisms that have been elaborated for the adsorption of organic pollutants by MOFs [109,112–115]. On the one hand, the presence of imidazole rings in the structure of ZIF-8 as a ligand can be one of the main reasons for the high adsorption capacity of ZIFs. Due to owing double bonds in imidazole rings, π–π stacking interactions with aromatic rings of malachite green occurred. This specific interaction enables ZIFs and their composite to adsorb the high capacity of MG [116]. Increased GQD functional group improved adsorption of the GQD@ZIF-8 composite. Carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the GQDs surface can have effective electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions with cationic malachite green structure. Indeed, the effect of functional groups of GQD and its quantum size can not be ignored [41]. Besides, the presence of double bonds in the GQDs structure has provided the proper conditions for the π–π stacking interactions [37]. The remaining PVP in the ZIF structure can be another factor in effective interactions in the adsorption phenomenon. Electrostatic interactions, π–π stacking interaction and hydrophobicity are among the significant interactions [117–120].

On the other hand, recently, different growth at the diverse surface of the MOFs crystal structure has been introduced as one of the momentous reasons for the disparate functions of the different morphologies of one type of MOF. In this work, in addition to the effect of GQD functional groups and their quantum size, the difference in crystal surface growth of the GQD @ ZIF-8 rhombic dodecahedral structure compared to the pristine ZIF-8 with a cubic structure may be another reason to justify their different adsorption rates [80,101,102].

Also, in several articles, ZIFs have been introduced as one of the flexible MOFs with the ability to breathe, this issue can strengthen this possibility of MG penetration in ZIFs pores [33,121]. So, the good capacity of ZIF-8 and its composite to adsorb malachite green may be due to the potential effect of ZIF-8 for breathing, which has been mentioned.

In
Scheme 2 , the possible adsorption mechanism between the adsorbent and the MG is shown [122–125].

The reusability of adsorbents is one of the requirements of their industrialization. To evaluate the recovery of the synthesized adsorbent, the adsorption test of 20 mg/L malachite green was repeated several times after rinsing the adsorbent with ethanol and drying it at 60 °C. As shown in Fig. 11, although the removal of malachite green by the GQD@ZIF-8 adsorbent slightly decreased after four consecutive cycles, at the end of the cycle, there was still 80% efficiency, which demonstrates the cost-effectiveness of the adsorbent and the efficiency of the recycling process [49,56].

The successful synthesis of GQD@ZIF-8 through a simple economy method at ambient temperature and improving the adsorption efficiency are the merits of this work. Despite the change in the specific morphology of ZIF-8 with increasing GQD on ZIF-8 to prepare nanocomposite, the structural framework remains intact and improvement in the adsorption process is observed. The presence of imidazole rings in the structure of ZIF-8 as an effective linker makes possible the accumulated interactions of π-π with malachite green aromatic rings.

Carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of GQDs enable electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding with cationic malachite green structure. Also, the presence of binary bonds in the structure of GQDs provides suitable conditions for the π-π interactions. the quantum size of GQDs makes it more efficient as well. The growth of different crystalline surfaces of a MOF type can lead to differences in the performance of its various morphologies. This issue is another justification for improving the performance of the composite over pure ZIF-8.
According to research, we found that the GQD @ ZIF-8 composite follows the Langmuir isotherm and is clearly described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model.
